title: Sunday roast chicken
subtitle: Buttered breast, lemon and thyme, pan gravy.
excerpt: A 1.8 kg bird, butter rubbed under the skin, lemon and thyme in the cavity, roasted on a bed of onion and carrot. Eighty minutes in a hot oven; twenty resting; pan gravy from the juices. Sunday lunch for four with leftovers for sandwiches.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), recipes 956 to 960 on roast fowl and gravy. Project Gutenberg #10136. Beeton's roast-fowl method (high heat to colour, basting through the cook) is the template the modern butter-under-the-skin recipe sharpens. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), chapter on roast poultry. Acton's emphasis on a clear, plain pan gravy made from the resting juices reads as modern today.

content.text: A 1.8 kg chicken roasted for eighty minutes feeds four with leftovers. Butter rubbed under the breast skin keeps the white meat moist. Lemon and thyme in the cavity scent the bird from the inside out. The roasting pan is set up with onion, carrot, and celery underneath, which lift the bird off the base and become the pan gravy when the cooking is done.

The two most useful things you can do to a roast chicken happen before it goes near the oven. The first is to leave the bird uncovered in the fridge overnight if you have the chance; the skin dries and crisps better in the oven. The second is to take it out of the fridge thirty minutes before roasting so it goes in close to room temperature; a cold bird roasts unevenly.

The oven starts hot at 220°C conventional (200°C fan) for the first twenty minutes to colour the skin. Then it drops to 200°C conventional (180°C fan) for the rest of the cook. The bird is done at an internal thigh temperature of 74°C. Pull it from the oven a few degrees early; the last few degrees climb on the board during the twenty-minute rest.

attrs.body: Most home cooks don't need to truss the bird. The argument for tying the legs together is presentation; the argument against is that tight thighs against the breast slow the dark meat down and dry the white meat out. Let the legs fall open. The breast cooks faster and stays juicier.
attrs.title: Skip the trussing

content.text: What you need

items.name: Whole chicken
items.prepNote: 1.6 to 1.8 kg, free range if possible

content.text: A heavy metal roasting pan around 30 by 23 cm holds the heat. A roasting rack helps if you have one, though the bed of vegetables does the same job of lifting the bird off the base of the pan. A thermometer with a probe takes the doubt out of doneness.
Method
Prepare the bird

Take the bird out of the fridge thirty minutes before it goes in the oven. Heat the oven to 220°C conventional, 200°C fan. Pat the skin dry with kitchen paper. Slide your fingers between the skin and the breast meat from the cavity end, working gently to loosen the skin without tearing it. Push softened {{unsalted-butter}} of butter under the skin and press the skin back down over the breast to spread it evenly.

Tuck the halved {{lemon}} lemon, {{thyme}} of thyme, and {{garlic}} of crushed garlic into the cavity. Rub the skin all over with {{olive-oil}} of olive oil and scatter generously with sea-salt flakes and freshly ground black pepper.
Build the bed

Scatter the {{onion}} halved onions, {{carrot}} chunks of carrot, and {{celery}} chunks of celery across the base of the roasting pan with {{bay-leaves}} of bay. Sit the bird breast-up on the vegetables.
Roast hot, then drop the heat

Slide the pan into the middle of the oven. Roast for twenty minutes at the full heat to colour the skin, then drop the temperature to 200°C conventional, 180°C fan. Roast for a further sixty minutes. A 1.8 kg bird will be done in around eighty minutes total; a 1.6 kg bird in seventy. Test with a thermometer in the thickest part of the thigh: 74°C means the bird is done.
Rest the bird

Lift the chicken out of the pan and rest on a board with a juice gutter for twenty minutes. The board catches the juices that come out of the bird as the meat relaxes; those juices go into the gravy. The bird won't go cold in twenty minutes, even uncovered; it stays plenty warm enough.
Make the gravy

While the bird rests, tip most of the fat out of the roasting pan into a heatproof jug, leaving the brown sticky bits and the vegetables. Set the pan over a medium heat on the hob. Sprinkle {{plain-flour}} of plain flour over the vegetables and stir for a minute to cook the flour out. Pour in {{white-wine-dry}} of dry white wine, scraping the base with a wooden spoon to lift the brown crust. Let the wine bubble for two minutes.

Pour in {{stock-chicken}} of chicken stock and any juices that have collected under the resting bird. Simmer for five minutes, stirring, until the gravy thickens enough to coat the back of a spoon. Strain through a fine-mesh sieve into a warmed jug, pressing the vegetables to extract every bit of liquid. Taste and adjust seasoning.
Carve

Pull the legs away from the body, cutting through the skin and the joint with the tip of a sharp knife. Separate the drumsticks from the thighs. Slice the breasts off the carcass in one piece each, then slice each breast across the grain into thick slices. Lay everything on a warm platter and pour over a little of the gravy; serve the rest in the jug.

attrs.intro: Most roast-chicken failures come down to oven temperature, skin moisture, or rest time. The fixes are usually small.

items.fix: Pull the bird from the oven at 72°C in the thigh and let the resting carry-over take it to 74°C. Butter under the breast skin buffers the white meat. Twenty minutes of rest matters; without it the juices run out of the slices instead of staying in the meat.
items.cause: Overcooked, or no rest, or no butter under the skin.

items.fix: Pat the skin dry with kitchen paper before rubbing on the oil and salt. The first twenty minutes at full heat is what crisps the skin; if your oven runs cool, go up another twenty degrees for that opening blast.
items.cause: Bird went in wet, or the oven wasn't hot enough at the start.
items.symptom: Skin pale and flabby rather than crisp and gold.

items.fix: Always rest the bird at room temperature for thirty minutes before it goes in the oven. A fridge-cold bird needs an extra ten minutes in the oven. If you slice and find pink, return the affected pieces to a low oven on a baking tray for five minutes.
items.cause: Heat couldn't reach the centre, often from a cold bird going in.
items.symptom: Underside of the breast pink at carving.

items.fix: Push the vegetables aside in the pan and let them catch a little before adding the flour; the brown is the flavour. Cook the flour for a full minute before any liquid goes in so the gravy thickens cleanly without raw-flour taste.
items.cause: Not enough brown stuck to the pan, or not enough flour cooked into the base.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting

content.text: Variations

Tarragon and lemon instead of thyme and lemon gives a French bistro flavour; the tarragon goes both in the cavity and in the butter under the skin. Garlic-and-rosemary butter is a heavier flavour that suits a winter table. For a richer gravy, add a glass of madeira in place of half the wine.
Make ahead, freezing, leftovers

The bird benefits from sitting unwrapped in the fridge overnight to dry the skin. The butter, lemon, and herbs can go on the morning of the roast; the bird then sits in the fridge until thirty minutes before the oven goes on. Pan gravy freezes well for three months in small tubs; defrost in the fridge and reheat gently. Cold chicken sandwiched between bread with mayonnaise and lettuce the next day is one of the better dishes you'll eat all week.
Where this dish lives

Roast chicken is the centre of the British Sunday table. The old Victorian method is a high-heat colour first, then a slower roast with regular basting. The modern butter-under-the-skin version sharpens it by buffering the breast meat against the long cook.

The gravy is a plain pan reduction from the roasting juices. Thicken it lightly with flour and lift it with a splash of wine. That's still the version that beats every shop-bought alternative. The Sunday roast survives because every part draws on every other: the bird flavours the gravy, the gravy flavours the potatoes, the leftovers feed lunch on Monday.
